MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(1): The paper reports no comparisons to any existing topic modeling or embedding-based baselines (summary states "Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so there are no meaningful empirical contrasts. Because of this omission I cannot assess fairness, matching data/metrics, or whether prior methods perform comparably; the paper therefore fails to analyze differences against established approaches. Although related work appears adequately cited and code is released, the lack of controlled baseline experiments makes the empirical claims unverifiable. | B(2): The paper introduces an interesting joint word/topic embedding model and reports coherence/NPMI metrics, but it presents no comparisons against established topic-modeling baselines (e.g., LDA variants, neural topic models) on the same datasets. While related work is discussed and code/hyperparameters are provided, the lack of baseline results means differences in performance and trade-offs are not empirically demonstrated or fairly evaluated. The paper does acknowledge some internal limitations (effects of the Dirichlet term), but it fails to show when prior methods perform comparably or better, making the empirical claims weak.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports no comparisons to any existing topic modeling or embedding-based baselines (summary states "Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so there are no meaningful empirical contrasts. Although related work appears adequately cited and code is released, the lack of controlled baseline experiments makes the empirical claims unverifiable.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces an interesting joint word/topic embedding model and reports coherence/NPMI metrics, but it presents no comparisons against established topic-modeling baselines (e.g., LDA variants, neural topic models) on the same datasets. While related work is discussed and code/hyperparameters are provided, the lack of baseline results means differences in performance and trade-offs are not empirically demonstrated or fairly evaluated. The paper does acknowledge some internal limitations (effects of the Dirichlet term), but it fails to show when prior methods perform comparably or better, making the empirical claims weak.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles a relevant problem—bridging probabilistic topic models and word embeddings to yield interpretable, sparse document-topic mixtures—and proposes a simple, jointly trained model (lda2vec) with a Dirichlet sparsity prior and open-source code, which makes it practically useful. However, the summary indicates no comparisons to established baselines and evaluation on only two corpora, which limits confidence that the results will fundamentally shift thinking across the field. The method is nevertheless a useful, implementable contribution that is likely to be built upon for applied and follow-up research rather than being immediately transformative. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical problem—combining dense word embeddings with interpretable, sparse document-topic mixtures—by proposing lda2vec, a simple jointly trained model that preserves word-vector linearities while yielding Dirichlet-regularized topic proportions and providing open-source code. The approach is immediately usable (implemented in an autodiff framework and reported on real corpora with coherence measures) and is likely to change how practitioners blend embedding-based representations with topic models and to spawn follow-up work, though its broader transformative impact is tempered by limited empirical comparisons to strong baselines in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a relevant problem—bridging probabilistic topic models and word embeddings to yield interpretable, sparse document-topic mixtures—and proposes a simple, jointly trained model (lda2vec) with a Dirichlet sparsity prior and open-source code, which makes it practically useful. However, the summary indicates no comparisons to established baselines and evaluation on only two corpora, which limits confidence that the results will fundamentally shift thinking across the field. The method is nevertheless a useful, implementable contribution that is likely to be built upon for applied and follow-up research rather than being immediately transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—combining dense word embeddings with interpretable, sparse document-topic mixtures—by proposing lda2vec, a simple jointly trained model that preserves word-vector linearities while yielding Dirichlet-regularized topic proportions and providing open-source code. The approach is immediately usable (implemented in an autodiff framework and reported on real corpora with coherence measures) and is likely to change how practitioners blend embedding-based representations with topic models and to spawn follow-up work, though its broader transformative impact is tempered by limited empirical comparisons to strong baselines in the summary.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): From the provided summary, the paper combines two well-established ideas (SGNS word embeddings and Dirichlet-topic document mixtures) into a single jointly-trained model and adds a sparsity-inducing Dirichlet regularizer and summed context vectors. This is a coherent and useful engineering integration that clearly differentiates itself from prior single-component methods and is easy to implement, but it largely composes existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. An expert would find the concrete joint-training and practical recipe informative, but the core ideas are incremental rather than highly original. | B(4): The paper combines established building blocks (SGNS word embeddings, document vectors, and Dirichlet sparsity from topic models) into a single, jointly trained model that produces interpretable sparse document-topic mixtures in the same embedding space as words — a clear and useful synthesis that is not trivially obvious. The formulation of context vectors as pivot-word plus document vectors together with an explicit Dirichlet regularizer on document-topic proportions is a non-trivial extension beyond standard skip-gram or doc2vec approaches and is clearly distinguished from prior work. While the core components are well-known, their integration to yield topic coherence and embedding-style linearities represents a notable and practical contribution that would interest experts.

reviewer_a.justification: From the provided summary, the paper combines two well-established ideas (SGNS word embeddings and Dirichlet-topic document mixtures) into a single jointly-trained model and adds a sparsity-inducing Dirichlet regularizer and summed context vectors. This is a coherent and useful engineering integration that clearly differentiates itself from prior single-component methods and is easy to implement, but it largely composes existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. An expert would find the concrete joint-training and practical recipe informative, but the core ideas are incremental rather than highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines established building blocks (SGNS word embeddings, document vectors, and Dirichlet sparsity from topic models) into a single, jointly trained model that produces interpretable sparse document-topic mixtures in the same embedding space as words — a clear and useful synthesis that is not trivially obvious. The formulation of context vectors as pivot-word plus document vectors together with an explicit Dirichlet regularizer on document-topic proportions is a non-trivial extension beyond standard skip-gram or doc2vec approaches and is clearly distinguished from prior work. While the core components are well-known, their integration to yield topic coherence and embedding-style linearities represents a notable and practical contribution that would interest experts.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents an interesting joint modeling approach and reports topic coherences on Two Newsgroups and qualitative results on Hacker News, and it provides code and hyperparameters which aids reproducibility. However, experiments lack controlled baselines (e.g., LDA, neural topic models) and statistical rigor (no seed variance or significance reported), and ablation analyses are minimal beyond a single Dirichlet α observation. Related work coverage is adequate and a limitation (α=1 failure) is noted, but failure modes and sensitivity (optimization, negative sampling, scalability) are insufficiently explored to fully support the claims. | B(3): The paper provides a clear methodology, reports coherent-topic metrics (Cv/NPMI) on Twenty Newsgroups and qualitative results on a Hacker News corpus, and supplies code and hyperparameters, so reproducibility is plausible. However, experimental coverage is limited: no baseline comparisons to established topic models or embedding-based hybrids are reported, and datasets are few. There is one sensible ablation (effect of the Dirichlet sparsity term) and some discussion of its failure mode, but broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., to topic count, negative sampling, initialization) and quantitative comparisons are missing. Related work is described adequately, but the empirical evaluation is only moderately comprehensive to fully support the claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents an interesting joint modeling approach and reports topic coherences on Two Newsgroups and qualitative results on Hacker News, and it provides code and hyperparameters which aids reproducibility. However, experiments lack controlled baselines (e.g., LDA, neural topic models) and statistical rigor (no seed variance or significance reported), and ablation analyses are minimal beyond a single Dirichlet α observation. Related work coverage is adequate and a limitation (α=1 failure) is noted, but failure modes and sensitivity (optimization, negative sampling, scalability) are insufficiently explored to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a clear methodology, reports coherent-topic metrics (Cv/NPMI) on Twenty Newsgroups and qualitative results on a Hacker News corpus, and supplies code and hyperparameters, so reproducibility is plausible. However, experimental coverage is limited: no baseline comparisons to established topic models or embedding-based hybrids are reported, and datasets are few. There is one sensible ablation (effect of the Dirichlet sparsity term) and some discussion of its failure mode, but broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., to topic count, negative sampling, initialization) and quantitative comparisons are missing. Related work is described adequately, but the empirical evaluation is only moderately comprehensive to fully support the claims.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper sits squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope—combining topic models and word embeddings addresses core representation-learning and interpretability concerns and will interest the venue’s audience. The problem formulation (Dirichlet-regularized, joint embedding of words/topics/documents) is appropriate and the use of standard corpora (20 Newsgroups, Hacker News), coherence/NPMI metrics, and released code meet expected practices. However, I flag methodological/evaluation weaknesses that temper fit: no comparisons to explicit baselines or ablations are reported (important for demonstrating advantage), and evaluation relies primarily on coherence measures without statistical robustness or variance across seeds. With stronger empirical controls and baseline comparisons this would be an excellent fit. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it tackles a core representation learning problem by combining word embeddings with interpretable topic mixtures and provides an open-source implementation. The formulation (jointly training word, topic, and document vectors with a Dirichlet sparsity prior) and use of coherence/NPMI metrics are appropriate for the community and audience. My main caveat is empirical rigor: the paper reports strong coherence numbers but compares to no explicit baselines, which weakens claims of improvement over existing topic models. Overall it is a good fit with minor concerns about comparative evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope—combining topic models and word embeddings addresses core representation-learning and interpretability concerns and will interest the venue’s audience. The problem formulation (Dirichlet-regularized, joint embedding of words/topics/documents) is appropriate and the use of standard corpora (20 Newsgroups, Hacker News), coherence/NPMI metrics, and released code meet expected practices. However, I flag methodological/evaluation weaknesses that temper fit: no comparisons to explicit baselines or ablations are reported (important for demonstrating advantage), and evaluation relies primarily on coherence measures without statistical robustness or variance across seeds. With stronger empirical controls and baseline comparisons this would be an excellent fit.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides many actionable details: hyperparameters are reported, the model is implemented in Chainer and open-sourced, optimization (Adam) and negative sampling are described, and evaluation metrics (Cv via Palmetto, NPMI, 3COSMUL) are specified. Public datasets (Twenty Newsgroups) are used and Hacker News is described, but the summary does not confirm complete preprocessing steps for Hacker News or precise negative-sampling/training schedules, random-seed variance, and some optimization/runtime details that are important for faithful reproduction. Given the available code and most hyperparameters, reproduction should be straightforward aside from a few implementation/training niceties and dataset preprocessing specifics. | B(4): Overall the paper provides many necessary reproducibility elements: hyperparameters are reported, the training ingredients (SGNS with negative sampling, Adam) and the Dirichlet regularizer are described, evaluation metrics (Cv via Palmetto, NPMI, 3COSMUL) are specified, and code is open-sourced in Chainer. However, the summary does not document the data preprocessing pipeline in detail (tokenization, stopword handling, vocabulary cutoff, handling of Hacker News), and some minor training/configuration choices (exact negative sampling scheme, initialization seeds, epoch counts or learning-rate schedules) are not fully enumerated in the summary. Given the available code and reported hyperparameters, the method is mostly reproducible with only minor missing details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides many actionable details: hyperparameters are reported, the model is implemented in Chainer and open-sourced, optimization (Adam) and negative sampling are described, and evaluation metrics (Cv via Palmetto, NPMI, 3COSMUL) are specified. Public datasets (Twenty Newsgroups) are used and Hacker News is described, but the summary does not confirm complete preprocessing steps for Hacker News or precise negative-sampling/training schedules, random-seed variance, and some optimization/runtime details that are important for faithful reproduction. Given the available code and most hyperparameters, reproduction should be straightforward aside from a few implementation/training niceties and dataset preprocessing specifics.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper provides many necessary reproducibility elements: hyperparameters are reported, the training ingredients (SGNS with negative sampling, Adam) and the Dirichlet regularizer are described, evaluation metrics (Cv via Palmetto, NPMI, 3COSMUL) are specified, and code is open-sourced in Chainer. However, the summary does not document the data preprocessing pipeline in detail (tokenization, stopword handling, vocabulary cutoff, handling of Hacker News), and some minor training/configuration choices (exact negative sampling scheme, initialization seeds, epoch counts or learning-rate schedules) are not fully enumerated in the summary. Given the available code and reported hyperparameters, the method is mostly reproducible with only minor missing details.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, with the main contributions (lda2vec, Dirichlet regularizer, joint training of word/topic/document vectors) stated early and clearly. Notation and model description are generally consistent and readable, and figures/tables/examples (including coherence metrics and analogy illustrations) effectively communicate results; hyperparameters are reported and code is released which aids reproducibility. Writing quality is good overall, though the experimental section could be presented more systematically (e.g., clearer structuring of reported metrics and comparisons) to further improve clarity for a reader trying to reproduce the work. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the intro, method, experiments and conclusion follow a logical flow and the main contributions (lda2vec, joint training of word/topic/document vectors, Dirichlet sparsity) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to reproduce the approach (hyperparameters and code are provided), and figures/tables/examples (coherences, analogies, datasets) effectively illustrate results. Small presentation blemishes remain — for example no baselines are compared in experiments which could have been flagged explicitly, and a few methodological details might benefit from tighter explanation — but the writing is otherwise good and free of major ambiguity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, with the main contributions (lda2vec, Dirichlet regularizer, joint training of word/topic/document vectors) stated early and clearly. Notation and model description are generally consistent and readable, and figures/tables/examples (including coherence metrics and analogy illustrations) effectively communicate results; hyperparameters are reported and code is released which aids reproducibility. Writing quality is good overall, though the experimental section could be presented more systematically (e.g., clearer structuring of reported metrics and comparisons) to further improve clarity for a reader trying to reproduce the work.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the intro, method, experiments and conclusion follow a logical flow and the main contributions (lda2vec, joint training of word/topic/document vectors, Dirichlet sparsity) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to reproduce the approach (hyperparameters and code are provided), and figures/tables/examples (coherences, analogies, datasets) effectively illustrate results. Small presentation blemishes remain — for example no baselines are compared in experiments which could have been flagged explicitly, and a few methodological details might benefit from tighter explanation — but the writing is otherwise good and free of major ambiguity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper clearly states its core claims and presents experiments showing coherent topics and word analogies, but supporting evidence is incomplete: no baseline topic models or ablation studies (e.g., effect of Dirichlet α, negative sampling choices, or separate training of components) and no statistical tests over multiple seeds. The mathematical modification to SGNS is straightforward and plausibly correct, but no formal analysis or proofs are provided and critical implementation/optimization details needed for reproducibility (negative sample strategy, initialization, convergence behavior, runtimes) are missing. Given these methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions about why summing document and word vectors yields interpretable, sparse topics, the conclusions are promising but not fully validated by the experiments presented. | B(3): The paper states clear, testable claims and presents experiments (Twenty Newsgroups, Hacker News) showing coherent topics and analogies, but it lacks comparisons to strong baselines and statistical controls, weakening support for claimed advantages. The mathematical formulation (augmenting SGNS with document vectors and a Dirichlet sparsity term) is straightforward and appears correct from the summary, but no proofs are required or missing. Key methodological concern: absence of baseline topic models or ablations and no reported statistical significance makes it hard to judge whether improvements are substantive rather than anecdotal. Overall, conclusions are plausible but not fully justified by the limited experimental methodology described.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its core claims and presents experiments showing coherent topics and word analogies, but supporting evidence is incomplete: no baseline topic models or ablation studies (e.g., effect of Dirichlet α, negative sampling choices, or separate training of components) and no statistical tests over multiple seeds. The mathematical modification to SGNS is straightforward and plausibly correct, but no formal analysis or proofs are provided and critical implementation/optimization details needed for reproducibility (negative sample strategy, initialization, convergence behavior, runtimes) are missing. Given these methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions about why summing document and word vectors yields interpretable, sparse topics, the conclusions are promising but not fully validated by the experiments presented.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and presents experiments (Twenty Newsgroups, Hacker News) showing coherent topics and analogies, but it lacks comparisons to strong baselines and statistical controls, weakening support for claimed advantages. The mathematical formulation (augmenting SGNS with document vectors and a Dirichlet sparsity term) is straightforward and appears correct from the summary, but no proofs are required or missing. Key methodological concern: absence of baseline topic models or ablations and no reported statistical significance makes it hard to judge whether improvements are substantive rather than anecdotal. Overall, conclusions are plausible but not fully justified by the limited experimental methodology described.

main_claims: Introduces lda2vec, which extends Skipgram Negative Sampling (SGNS) by combining word embeddings with interpretable, sparse document-to-topic mixtures via a Dirichlet prior.

Jointly trains word, topic, and document vectors in a common embedding space where context vectors are the sum of pivot word and document vectors, preserving semantic linearities of word vectors while producing sparse document-topic proportions.
Produces coherent and interpretable topics (reported high mean topic coherences on Twenty Newsgroups) and discovers salient topics and word analogies in a Hacker News comments corpus.
The method is simple to implement in automatic-differentiation frameworks (implemented in Chainer) and is open-sourced.
_summary.methodology: Modify the SGNS objective by forming context vectors as the sum of pivot word and document vectors and add a Dirichlet-likelihood regularizer on document-topic proportions to encourage sparsity; train word, topic, and document vectors jointly with negative sampling and Adam optimization.
metrics: Cv topic coherence (via Palmetto, based on NPMI)
limitations: When the sparsity-inducing Dirichlet term is not used (e.g., α=1), document weights spread across topics and topics become incoherent.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - topic modeling and word embeddings

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a research background in probabilistic models and large-scale optimization; experienced in implementing and benchmarking representation learning systems for NLP.
traits: insists on clear baselines, ablation studies, and controlled experiments to support empirical claims
looks for complete implementation details (hyperparameters, training schedules, initialization, negative sampling settings) to enable reproducibility
evaluates statistical rigor: significance, variance across random seeds, and sensitivity to priors and regularizers
focuses on optimization and computational aspects: convergence behavior, runtime/memory cost, and scalability to larger corpora
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Carefully interrogates experimental design and reproducibility, asking for ablations and implementation details that validate each claimed advantage of the method.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Senior Scientist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in NLP with broad experience in topic modeling, word embeddings, and interpretability; tracks practical needs and conceptual advances in unsupervised text representations.
traits: assesses novelty relative to existing topic models and embedding-based approaches and how the contribution advances the field
values interpretability and usefulness of learned topics for downstream analysis and real applications
examines qualitative behavior (topic coherence, analogies) and whether reported metrics align with perceived practical utility
considers broader impact, ease of adoption (e.g., open-source, implementation in common frameworks), and limitations in realistic settings
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper by situating it within prior work and judging whether the proposed approach provides a meaningful, usable advance for researchers and practitioners.
